\begin{document}

\begin{center}
    {\LARGE \bfseries Fourier Series and Partial Differential Equations}\\[6pt]
    {\large Prepared by Staples Education}\\[4pt]
    {\itshape Unit 18: From Fourier Series to Separation of Variables and the Classical PDEs}
\end{center}

\vspace{0.4em}

\tableofcontents
\vspace{0.4em}

\begin{topicsbox}[Unit 18 at a Glance: Fourier Series as the Key to Partial Differential Equations]
A \textcolor{accentorange}{Fourier series} expresses a periodic function as a sum of sines and cosines, and because the trigonometric system is \emph{orthogonal}, every coefficient is one independent integral --- the \textcolor{accentorange}{Euler--Fourier formulas}. Symmetry simplifies the work: \textcolor{accentorange}{even} functions need only cosines, \textcolor{accentorange}{odd} functions only sines, and a function on a half-interval can be expanded as either via a half-range extension. This is the toolkit that cracks \textcolor{accentorange}{partial differential equations}: \textcolor{accentorange}{separation of variables} $u=X(x)T(t)$ splits a PDE into ODEs linked by a separation constant, the boundary conditions reproduce the eigenvalue BVP of Unit~17, and the initial profile is matched by a Fourier series. The \textcolor{accentorange}{Laplacian} $\nabla^2 u$ governs the three classical equations --- heat (diffusion), wave (vibration), and Laplace (steady state).

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=1.4em, itemsep=3pt]
    \item \textbf{Fourier intuition} --- any reasonable periodic signal is a superposition of pure frequencies; the Fourier transform is the continuous-spectrum limit of the Fourier series.
    \item \textbf{Computing the series} --- $a_n=\frac1L\int_{-L}^{L}f\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx$, $b_n=\frac1L\int_{-L}^{L}f\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx$, derived from trig orthogonality.
    \item \textbf{Symmetry and half-range} --- even $\Rightarrow$ cosine series, odd $\Rightarrow$ sine series; extend a function on $0\le x\le L$ evenly or oddly to get either.
    \item \textbf{Separation of variables} --- $u(x,t)=X(x)T(t)$ turns a linear homogeneous PDE into two ODEs; the spatial ODE is the Unit~17 eigenvalue problem.
    \item \textbf{The Laplacian and the classical PDEs} --- $\nabla^2 u=u_{xx}+u_{yy}$ drives heat $u_t=\alpha^2 u_{xx}$, wave $u_{tt}=c^2 u_{xx}$, and Laplace $\nabla^2 u=0$.
\end{itemize}
\end{topicsbox}

\vspace{0.6em}

\setcounter{section}{18}

% ---- Module 18.1 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Fourier Series and Transform Intuition}
\emph{A periodic function as a superposition of pure-frequency sines and cosines; the time-domain versus frequency-domain picture; harmonics and the fundamental frequency; the Fourier transform as the continuous-spectrum limit of the Fourier series.}

\begin{formulabox}[The Fourier Series --- Superposition of Harmonics]
\[ f(x) = \frac{a_0}{2} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left( a_n\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L} + b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\right), \qquad \text{period } 2L. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Harmonics and the Fundamental Frequency]
\[ \begin{gathered} \text{the } n\text{th harmonic has frequency } \tfrac{n}{2L}; \\ n=1 \text{ is the \emph{fundamental}},\ n\ge 2 \text{ the \emph{overtones}.} \end{gathered} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[The Fourier Transform --- Continuous Spectrum]
\[ \begin{gathered} \hat f(\xi) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x)\,e^{-2\pi i \xi x}\,dx; \\ \text{the } L\to\infty \text{ limit turns the discrete harmonics } n/2L \text{ into a continuum } \xi. \end{gathered} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Read a Function in the Frequency Domain]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Treat the signal $f(x)$ as living in the \emph{time} (or space) domain --- its value at each point.
    \item Ask which pure frequencies $\cos(n\pi x/L),\ \sin(n\pi x/L)$ it is built from, and with what amplitudes $a_n,b_n$.
    \item Plot the amplitudes against frequency: this \emph{frequency-domain} spectrum is an equivalent description of the same signal.
    \item For a non-periodic signal, let the period $2L\to\infty$; the discrete spectrum fills in to a continuous one and the sum becomes the Fourier transform integral.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- Spectrum of a Two-Tone Signal]
Identify the Fourier content of $f(x)=3+5\cos(\pi x)-2\sin(3\pi x)$ on the interval of period $2$ (so $L=1$).
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Match the template: } f(x)=\frac{a_0}{2}+\sum_n\big(a_n\cos n\pi x+b_n\sin n\pi x\big) \text{ with } L=1.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Constant term: } \frac{a_0}{2}=3 \Rightarrow a_0=6 \text{ (the average / DC level).}} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Cosine amplitude: } a_1=5 \text{ (a fundamental cosine at frequency } \tfrac12).} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Sine amplitude: } b_3=-2 \text{ (a third-harmonic sine); all other } a_n,b_n=0.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Spectrum: } \text{only three lines --- DC, } n=1 \text{ cosine, } n=3 \text{ sine --- because } f \text{ is already a finite trig sum.}} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
A Fourier series says any reasonable periodic function is a \textcolor{accentorange}{superposition of pure frequencies} --- a chord of sines and cosines. The same signal then has two equivalent portraits: the familiar \textcolor{accentorange}{time-domain} graph and the \textcolor{accentorange}{frequency-domain} spectrum of amplitudes. Letting the period grow without bound turns the discrete spectrum into a continuous one, and the series becomes the Fourier transform.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
The constant term is $a_0/2$, \textcolor{warnred}{not} $a_0$ --- the factor of $\tfrac12$ is what makes the single Euler--Fourier formula $a_n=\frac1L\int_{-L}^{L}f\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx$ work for $n=0$ as well. Also, a Fourier \emph{series} needs a \textcolor{warnred}{periodic} (or finite-interval) function; a non-periodic signal on the whole line requires the Fourier \emph{transform}, not a series.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

% ---- Module 18.2 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Computing Fourier Series}
\emph{The Euler--Fourier coefficient formulas on $-L\le x\le L$; orthogonality of the trigonometric system as their engine; the constant (DC) term $a_0/2$; convergence at jumps to the midpoint of the one-sided limits (Dirichlet conditions).}

\begin{formulabox}[The Euler--Fourier Coefficient Formulas]
\[ a_n = \frac{1}{L}\int_{-L}^{L} f(x)\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx \ (n\ge 0), \qquad b_n = \frac{1}{L}\int_{-L}^{L} f(x)\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx \ (n\ge 1). \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Orthogonality of the Trigonometric System]
\[ \int_{-L}^{L}\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\cos\frac{m\pi x}{L}\,dx = L\,\delta_{nm}, \quad \int_{-L}^{L}\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\sin\frac{m\pi x}{L}\,dx = L\,\delta_{nm}, \quad \int_{-L}^{L}\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\sin\frac{m\pi x}{L}\,dx = 0. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Convergence at a Jump (Dirichlet)]
\[ \text{At a jump } x_0,\ \text{the series converges to } \tfrac12\big[f(x_0^-)+f(x_0^+)\big], \text{ the midpoint of the one-sided limits.} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Compute a Fourier Series]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Identify the half-period $L$ so the function repeats with period $2L$, and write the target $f=\frac{a_0}{2}+\sum_n(a_n\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}+b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L})$.
    \item Compute $a_0=\frac1L\int_{-L}^{L}f\,dx$ --- twice the average value of $f$ over one period.
    \item Compute $a_n$ and $b_n$ from the Euler--Fourier integrals, using integration by parts where $f$ is polynomial times trig.
    \item Exploit symmetry to skip half the work: if $f$ is even all $b_n=0$; if $f$ is odd all $a_n=0$ (and $a_0=0$).
    \item State the series and note its value at any jump is the midpoint $\tfrac12[f(x_0^-)+f(x_0^+)]$.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- Fourier Series of $f(x)=x$ on $-\pi\le x\le \pi$]
Find the Fourier series of the $2\pi$-periodic extension of $f(x)=x$ on $-\pi\le x\le\pi$ (so $L=\pi$), computing every coefficient in full.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Symmetry: } f(x)=x \text{ is odd} \Rightarrow a_0=0 \text{ and } a_n=0 \text{ for all } n; \text{ only the } b_n \text{ survive.}} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Set up } b_n: \ b_n=\frac1\pi\int_{-\pi}^{\pi} x\sin(nx)\,dx=\frac2\pi\int_{0}^{\pi} x\sin(nx)\,dx \ \text{(even integrand).}} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{By parts: } u=x,\ dv=\sin(nx)\,dx \Rightarrow du=dx,\ v=-\tfrac1n\cos(nx).} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\int_0^\pi x\sin(nx)\,dx=\Big[-\tfrac{x}{n}\cos(nx)\Big]_0^\pi+\tfrac1n\int_0^\pi\cos(nx)\,dx=-\tfrac{\pi}{n}\cos(n\pi)+\tfrac1{n^2}\big[\sin(nx)\big]_0^\pi.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Evaluate: } \cos(n\pi)=(-1)^n,\ \sin(n\pi)=0 \Rightarrow \int_0^\pi x\sin(nx)\,dx=-\tfrac{\pi}{n}(-1)^n=(-1)^{n+1}\tfrac{\pi}{n}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Coefficient: } b_n=\frac2\pi\cdot(-1)^{n+1}\tfrac{\pi}{n}=(-1)^{n+1}\frac{2}{n}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Series: } x=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}(-1)^{n+1}\frac{2}{n}\sin(nx)=2\Big(\sin x-\tfrac12\sin 2x+\tfrac13\sin 3x-\cdots\Big),\ \text{converging to } 0 \text{ at } x=\pm\pi.} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
The Euler--Fourier formulas are pure \textcolor{accentorange}{orthogonality}: integrating $f$ against a single basis wave annihilates every other term and leaves one coefficient. The constant $a_0/2$ is the function's \textcolor{accentorange}{average}; the $a_n,b_n$ are how strongly each harmonic is present. Symmetry is the great labor-saver --- recognizing $f$ as even or odd erases half the integrals before you compute anything.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
Watch the normalizations: the coefficients use $\frac1L$ (over the full period $2L$), while the half-range formulas of the next module use $\frac2L$ (over $0\le x\le L$). \textcolor{warnred}{Mixing the two} is the most common Fourier error. And the series equals $f$ only where $f$ is continuous --- at a \textcolor{warnred}{jump} it converges to the midpoint $\tfrac12[f(x_0^-)+f(x_0^+)]$, not to either one-sided value.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

% ---- Module 18.3 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Fourier Series for Differential Equations}
\emph{Even and odd functions and their product rules; the pure cosine series (even) and pure sine series (odd); half-range expansions via even or odd extension of a function given only on $0\le x\le L$; choosing the expansion that matches the boundary conditions of a PDE.}

\begin{formulabox}[Even/Odd Symmetry Collapses the Series]
\[ f \text{ even} \Rightarrow b_n=0 \ (\text{pure cosine series}); \qquad f \text{ odd} \Rightarrow a_n=0 \ (\text{pure sine series}, \text{ and } a_0=0). \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Half-Range Cosine Series (Even Extension)]
\[ f(x)=\frac{a_0}{2}+\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}, \qquad a_n=\frac{2}{L}\int_0^L f(x)\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx \ \ (n\ge 0). \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Half-Range Sine Series (Odd Extension)]
\[ f(x)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}, \qquad b_n=\frac{2}{L}\int_0^L f(x)\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Build a Half-Range Expansion]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item You are given $f$ only on $0\le x\le L$ and must represent it by sines \emph{or} cosines alone.
    \item For a \emph{sine} series, extend $f$ as an \emph{odd} function to $-L\le x\le 0$; for a \emph{cosine} series, extend it as an \emph{even} function.
    \item Apply the half-range formula with the $\frac2L$ normalization (the $-L$ to $0$ contribution doubles the $0$ to $L$ integral).
    \item Let the boundary conditions of the target PDE pick the series: Dirichlet ends ($u=0$) call for the \emph{sine} series, Neumann ends ($u_x=0$) for the \emph{cosine} series.
    \item Write the expansion, remembering the odd extension forces $f(0)=f(L)=0$ in the limit, matching homogeneous Dirichlet data.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- Half-Range Sine Series of $f(x)=1$ on $0\le x\le L$]
Expand the constant $f(x)=1$ on $0\le x\le L$ in a half-range \emph{sine} series (odd extension), the form needed for a Dirichlet BVP.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Formula: } b_n=\frac2L\int_0^L 1\cdot\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Integrate: } \int_0^L\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx=\Big[-\frac{L}{n\pi}\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\Big]_0^L=-\frac{L}{n\pi}\big(\cos n\pi-1\big).} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Simplify: } \cos n\pi=(-1)^n \Rightarrow \int_0^L\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx=\frac{L}{n\pi}\big(1-(-1)^n\big).} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Coefficient: } b_n=\frac2L\cdot\frac{L}{n\pi}\big(1-(-1)^n\big)=\frac{2}{n\pi}\big(1-(-1)^n\big)=\begin{cases}\dfrac{4}{n\pi}, & n \text{ odd},\\[4pt] 0, & n \text{ even}.\end{cases}} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Series: } 1=\frac{4}{\pi}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{2k+1}\sin\frac{(2k+1)\pi x}{L}=\frac4\pi\Big(\sin\frac{\pi x}{L}+\tfrac13\sin\frac{3\pi x}{L}+\cdots\Big),\ 0<x<L.} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
Symmetry is a choice you can \textcolor{accentorange}{impose}. A function living only on $0\le x\le L$ has no symmetry of its own, but you may extend it \textcolor{accentorange}{evenly} to get a cosine series or \textcolor{accentorange}{oddly} to get a sine series. Which you pick is dictated by the PDE: \textcolor{accentorange}{Dirichlet} boundary data wants the \textcolor{accentorange}{sine} series (it vanishes at the ends), \textcolor{accentorange}{Neumann} data wants the \textcolor{accentorange}{cosine} series (its slope vanishes).
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
The half-range formulas carry $\frac2L$, \textcolor{warnred}{not} $\frac1L$ --- the odd or even extension doubles the integral over $0\le x\le L$. Do not also integrate over $-L$ to $L$; that double-counts. And the sine series of a function with $f(0)\neq 0$ (like $f=1$ above) \textcolor{warnred}{converges to $0$ at the endpoints} because the odd extension forces a jump there --- expected, not an error.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

% ---- Module 18.4 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{Separable Partial Differential Equations}
\emph{The separation-of-variables ansatz $u(x,t)=X(x)T(t)$; splitting a linear homogeneous PDE into two ODEs joined by a separation constant; the role of homogeneous boundary conditions in producing the spatial eigenvalue BVP; the classical PDEs and their boundary/initial data.}

\begin{formulabox}[The Separation Ansatz and Constant]
\[ \begin{gathered} u(x,t)=X(x)\,T(t); \\ \frac{1}{\alpha^2}\frac{T'}{T}=\frac{X''}{X}=-\lambda \ \ (\text{constant: each side depends on a different variable}). \end{gathered} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[The Two Resulting ODEs (Heat Equation)]
\[ X''+\lambda X=0 \ \ (\text{spatial, an eigenvalue BVP}); \qquad T'+\alpha^2\lambda T=0 \ \ (\text{temporal, first order, } T_n=e^{-\alpha^2\lambda_n t}). \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Superposition of the Product Solutions]
\[ \begin{gathered} u(x,t)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} b_n\,X_n(x)\,T_n(t); \\ \text{the } b_n \text{ are fixed by matching the initial condition with a Fourier series.} \end{gathered} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Solve a PDE by Separation of Variables]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Assume a product solution $u(x,t)=X(x)T(t)$ and substitute into the PDE.
    \item Divide through so all $x$-dependence is on one side and all $t$-dependence on the other; set both equal to the separation constant $-\lambda$.
    \item Solve the \emph{spatial} ODE with the homogeneous boundary conditions first --- this is the Unit~17 eigenvalue BVP, yielding eigenvalues $\lambda_n$ and eigenfunctions $X_n$.
    \item Solve the \emph{temporal} ODE for each $\lambda_n$ to get $T_n(t)$, then form the superposition $u=\sum_n b_n X_n T_n$.
    \item Impose the initial condition $u(x,0)=f(x)$ and read off the $b_n$ as the Fourier (half-range) coefficients of $f$.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- Separating the Heat Equation]
Separate $u_t=\alpha^2 u_{xx}$ on $0\le x\le L$ with $u(0,t)=u(L,t)=0$, and identify the two ODEs and the spatial eigenpairs.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Substitute } u=X(x)T(t):\ XT'=\alpha^2 X''T.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Divide by } \alpha^2 XT:\ \frac{T'}{\alpha^2 T}=\frac{X''}{X}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Separation constant: } \text{LHS depends only on } t, \text{ RHS only on } x \Rightarrow \text{both} =-\lambda.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Spatial BVP: } X''+\lambda X=0,\ X(0)=X(L)=0 \Rightarrow \lambda_n=\left(\frac{n\pi}{L}\right)^2,\ X_n=\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L} \ (\text{Unit 17}).} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Temporal ODE: } T'+\alpha^2\lambda_n T=0 \Rightarrow T_n(t)=e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2 t}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Product modes: } u_n(x,t)=\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2 t},\ \text{ready to superpose.}} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
Separation of variables is the master move for linear PDEs: assuming $u=X(x)T(t)$ \textcolor{accentorange}{decouples} the equation into two ODEs tied by a single separation constant. The homogeneous boundary conditions turn the spatial ODE into the \textcolor{accentorange}{eigenvalue BVP of Unit~17}, so its eigenfunctions become the building blocks; \textcolor{accentorange}{superposition} assembles them and the \textcolor{accentorange}{initial condition} fixes the weights through a Fourier series.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
Separation of variables needs the PDE to be \textcolor{warnred}{linear and homogeneous with homogeneous boundary conditions} --- a nonzero boundary value or a forcing term breaks the clean split (handle those by first subtracting a steady-state/particular solution). Also, always solve the \textcolor{warnred}{spatial} eigenvalue problem before the temporal one: the boundary conditions live in $x$, and they are what quantize $\lambda$.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

% ---- Module 18.5 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{The Laplacian}
\emph{The Laplacian $\nabla^2 u=\nabla\cdot\nabla u=u_{xx}+u_{yy}$ as the divergence of the gradient; its meaning as how a value departs from its local average; Laplace's equation $\nabla^2 u=0$ and harmonic functions; the steady-state limit of diffusion; polar/spherical forms.}

\begin{formulabox}[The Laplacian --- Divergence of the Gradient]
\[ \nabla^2 u = \nabla\cdot(\nabla u) = \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}+\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}+\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} \ \ (\text{Cartesian, here through } 3\text{D}). \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Laplace's Equation and Harmonic Functions]
\[ \begin{gathered} \nabla^2 u = 0 \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad u \text{ is \emph{harmonic}}; \\ \text{value at a point} = \text{average over any surrounding circle/sphere (mean value).} \end{gathered} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[The Laplacian in Polar Coordinates]
\[ \nabla^2 u = u_{rr} + \frac{1}{r}u_r + \frac{1}{r^2}u_{\theta\theta} \ \ (\text{the natural form for disks and annuli}). \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Test and Interpret the Laplacian]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Compute the second partial in each spatial variable and add them to form $\nabla^2 u$.
    \item Read the sign as curvature versus the neighborhood: $\nabla^2 u>0$ means the value sits \emph{below} its local average (a dip), $\nabla^2 u<0$ means \emph{above} (a bump).
    \item If $\nabla^2 u=0$ everywhere, the function is harmonic --- it equals its local average and has no interior maxima or minima (maximum principle).
    \item For diffusion, recognize $\nabla^2 u$ as the spatial driver: heat flows so as to erase departures from the average, and the steady state ($u_t=0$) is exactly Laplace's equation.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- Verify a Harmonic Function]
Show that $u(x,y)=x^2-y^2$ is harmonic, and contrast it with $v(x,y)=x^2+y^2$.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Gradient pieces: } u_x=2x,\ u_{xx}=2; \qquad u_y=-2y,\ u_{yy}=-2.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Laplacian of } u: \ \nabla^2 u=u_{xx}+u_{yy}=2+(-2)=0 \Rightarrow u \text{ is harmonic.}} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Now } v: \ v_{xx}=2,\ v_{yy}=2 \Rightarrow \nabla^2 v=4\neq 0 \Rightarrow v \text{ is \emph{not} harmonic.}} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Interpretation: } \nabla^2 v=4>0 \Rightarrow v \text{ sits below its local average --- a bowl with an interior minimum.}} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Contrast: } u=x^2-y^2 \text{ is a saddle; its curvatures cancel, so no interior extremum (maximum principle).}} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
The Laplacian $\nabla^2 u$ is the \textcolor{accentorange}{divergence of the gradient} --- a scalar measuring how a point's value departs from the \textcolor{accentorange}{average of its neighbors}. Where it is positive the point is a relative dip, where negative a relative bump, and where it is \textcolor{accentorange}{zero the function is harmonic} and equals its local average. This single operator is the spatial engine of all three classical PDEs.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
The Laplacian is a \emph{sum} of unmixed second partials, $u_{xx}+u_{yy}$ --- \textcolor{warnred}{do not include the mixed partial} $u_{xy}$, and do not confuse $\nabla^2 u$ (a scalar) with the gradient $\nabla u$ (a vector) or the Hessian (a matrix). In curved coordinates you \textcolor{warnred}{cannot} just sum naive second derivatives: polar form carries the extra $\frac1r u_r$ and $\frac1{r^2}u_{\theta\theta}$ terms.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

% ---- Module 18.6 --------------------------------------------------
\subsection{The Heat and Wave Equations}
\emph{The heat equation $u_t=\alpha^2 u_{xx}$ (parabolic diffusion) solved by separation plus a Fourier sine series; the wave equation $u_{tt}=c^2 u_{xx}$ (hyperbolic vibration) with standing-mode and d'Alembert traveling-wave solutions; Duhamel's principle for a forced wave; the classification of the three classical equations.}

\begin{formulabox}[The Three Classical Equations]
\[ \begin{aligned} \text{Heat (parabolic): }\ &u_t=\alpha^2 u_{xx}; \\ \text{Wave (hyperbolic): }\ &u_{tt}=c^2 u_{xx}; \\ \text{Laplace (elliptic): }\ &u_{xx}+u_{yy}=0. \end{aligned} \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Heat Equation --- Fourier Solution]
\[ u(x,t)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2 t}, \qquad b_n=\frac{2}{L}\int_0^L f(x)\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx. \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{formulabox}[Wave Equation --- d'Alembert's Solution]
\[ u(x,t)=\tfrac12\big[f(x-ct)+f(x+ct)\big]+\frac{1}{2c}\int_{x-ct}^{x+ct} g(s)\,ds \quad (\text{initial shape } f,\ \text{initial velocity } g). \]
\end{formulabox}

\begin{methodbox}[Method --- Solve the 1D Heat Equation on a Rod]
\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=1.6em]
    \item Separate $u=X(x)T(t)$ and solve the spatial BVP $X''+\lambda X=0$, $X(0)=X(L)=0$ to get $\lambda_n=(n\pi/L)^2$, $X_n=\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}$.
    \item Solve $T'+\alpha^2\lambda_n T=0$ for $T_n=e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2 t}$, so each mode decays faster the higher its frequency.
    \item Superpose: $u(x,t)=\sum_n b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2 t}$.
    \item Set $t=0$: $u(x,0)=\sum_n b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}=f(x)$, so the $b_n$ are the half-range \emph{sine} coefficients of the initial temperature $f$.
    \item Evaluate $b_n=\frac2L\int_0^L f\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx$ and write the full series solution.
\end{enumerate}
\end{methodbox}

\begin{examplebox}[Worked Example --- Heat on a Rod with Initial Temperature $f(x)=100$]
A rod $0\le x\le L$ has ends held at $0^\circ$ ($u(0,t)=u(L,t)=0$) and uniform initial temperature $u(x,0)=100$. Solve $u_t=\alpha^2 u_{xx}$ completely, computing the coefficients in full.
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Separate: } u=X T \Rightarrow X''+\lambda X=0,\ X(0)=X(L)=0,\ \text{ and } T'+\alpha^2\lambda T=0.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Spatial eigenpairs: } \lambda_n=\left(\frac{n\pi}{L}\right)^2,\quad X_n=\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L},\quad n=1,2,3,\dots} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Temporal factor: } T_n(t)=e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2 t}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Superposition: } u(x,t)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2 t}.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Match } t=0: \ \sum_n b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}=100 \Rightarrow b_n=\frac2L\int_0^L 100\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx.} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Integrate: } \int_0^L\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx=\frac{L}{n\pi}\big(1-(-1)^n\big) \Rightarrow b_n=\frac{200}{n\pi}\big(1-(-1)^n\big).} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Odd-only: } b_n=\dfrac{400}{n\pi}\ (n \text{ odd}),\ 0\ (n \text{ even}).} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\textbf{Solution: } u(x,t)=\frac{400}{\pi}\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\frac{1}{2k+1}\sin\frac{(2k+1)\pi x}{L}\,e^{-\alpha^2((2k+1)\pi/L)^2 t}\ \xrightarrow{t\to\infty}\ 0.} \]
\end{examplebox}

\begin{conceptbox}[Key Takeaway]
The classical PDEs all yield to the same pipeline: \textcolor{accentorange}{separate}, solve the \textcolor{accentorange}{spatial eigenvalue BVP}, attach the time factor, \textcolor{accentorange}{superpose}, and fit the initial data with a \textcolor{accentorange}{Fourier series}. Their characters differ --- the \textcolor{accentorange}{heat} equation smooths and decays (high modes die fastest), the \textcolor{accentorange}{wave} equation oscillates and propagates (d'Alembert's two traveling waves), and \textcolor{accentorange}{Laplace} gives the steady state --- but Fourier analysis is the common engine.
\end{conceptbox}

\begin{warningbox}[Common Pitfall]
Match the time factor to the equation's order in $t$: the heat equation is \emph{first} order so each mode carries a single decaying $e^{-\alpha^2\lambda_n t}$, while the wave equation is \emph{second} order so each mode needs \textcolor{warnred}{two} time functions $\cos(c\sqrt{\lambda_n}\,t)$ and $\sin(c\sqrt{\lambda_n}\,t)$ --- and therefore \emph{two} initial conditions (shape \emph{and} velocity). Using a single exponential for the wave equation \textcolor{warnred}{loses half the solution}.
\end{warningbox}

\vspace{0.8em}

\clearpage
\phantomsection
\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{Practice Problems}
\section*{Practice Problems}

Work the following ten problems in order. They climb from reading the harmonics of a signal and computing Euler--Fourier coefficients, through even/odd symmetry and half-range expansions, into separation of variables and the Laplacian, and up to a complete separation-of-variables solution of the heat equation with every coefficient integral expanded, plus the d'Alembert solution of the wave equation. Complete solutions for all problems follow.

\begin{enumerate}[leftmargin=2em]
    \item \textbf{(Harmonics)} For $f(x)=4-3\cos(2\pi x)+\sin(\pi x)$ with period $2$ ($L=1$), state $a_0$, the nonzero $a_n$, and the nonzero $b_n$.

    \item \textbf{(Euler--Fourier setup)} Write the Euler--Fourier formulas for $a_n$ and $b_n$ of a $2L$-periodic function, and state the orthogonality relations that justify them.

    \item \textbf{(Odd function series)} Find the Fourier series of the $2\pi$-periodic extension of $f(x)=x$ on $-\pi\le x\le\pi$, computing $b_n$ in full.

    \item \textbf{(Symmetry shortcut)} Without integrating, explain why $f(x)=x^2$ on $-\pi\le x\le\pi$ has $b_n=0$ for all $n$, and which series type it has.

    \item \textbf{(Half-range sine)} Expand $f(x)=1$ on $0\le x\le L$ in a half-range sine series, giving $b_n$ and the resulting series.

    \item \textbf{(Separation of variables)} Separate $u_t=\alpha^2 u_{xx}$ via $u=X(x)T(t)$; state the two ODEs and, with $u(0,t)=u(L,t)=0$, the spatial eigenpairs and time factor.

    \item \textbf{(Laplacian / harmonic)} Determine whether $u(x,y)=x^2-y^2$ and $v(x,y)=e^{x}\sin y$ are harmonic by computing $\nabla^2$ of each.

    \item \textbf{(Classification)} Classify $u_t=\alpha^2u_{xx}$, $u_{tt}=c^2u_{xx}$, and $u_{xx}+u_{yy}=0$ as parabolic, hyperbolic, or elliptic, and name the phenomenon each models.

    \item \textbf{(Heat equation capstone)} Solve $u_t=\alpha^2u_{xx}$ on $0\le x\le L$, $u(0,t)=u(L,t)=0$, $u(x,0)=100$, computing the coefficients in full.

    \item \textbf{(Wave equation)} Use d'Alembert's formula to solve $u_{tt}=c^2u_{xx}$ on the line with $u(x,0)=f(x)$ and $u_t(x,0)=0$, and describe the motion.

\end{enumerate}

\clearpage
\phantomsection
\addcontentsline{toc}{section}{Complete Solutions}
\section*{Complete Solutions}

\noindent\textbf{Solution 1.}\quad Match $f=\frac{a_0}{2}+\sum_n(a_n\cos n\pi x+b_n\sin n\pi x)$ with $L=1$. The constant $\tfrac{a_0}{2}=4$, so
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{a_0=8; \qquad a_2=-3 \ (\text{from } -3\cos 2\pi x); \qquad b_1=1 \ (\text{from } \sin\pi x).} \]
All other coefficients are $0$.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 2.}\quad For a $2L$-periodic $f$,
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{a_n=\frac1L\int_{-L}^{L}f\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx,\qquad b_n=\frac1L\int_{-L}^{L}f\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx.} \]
They follow from orthogonality: $\int_{-L}^{L}\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\cos\frac{m\pi x}{L}dx=L\delta_{nm}$, the same for sines, and $\int_{-L}^{L}\cos\frac{n\pi x}{L}\sin\frac{m\pi x}{L}dx=0$ --- integrating $f$ against one basis wave isolates its coefficient.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 3.}\quad $f(x)=x$ is odd, so $a_n=0$ and only $b_n$ survive. With $L=\pi$, integration by parts gives
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{b_n=\frac1\pi\int_{-\pi}^{\pi}x\sin(nx)\,dx=\frac2\pi\Big[-\frac{\pi}{n}(-1)^n\Big]=(-1)^{n+1}\frac2n.} \]
Hence $x=\sum_{n\ge1}(-1)^{n+1}\frac2n\sin(nx)=2(\sin x-\tfrac12\sin2x+\tfrac13\sin3x-\cdots)$.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 4.}\quad $f(x)=x^2$ is \emph{even} ($f(-x)=f(x)$). The integrand $x^2\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}$ is then odd, so
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{b_n=\frac1L\int_{-L}^{L}x^2\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx=0 \quad(\text{odd integrand over a symmetric interval}).} \]
It therefore has a pure \emph{cosine} series (only $a_0$ and $a_n$).

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 5.}\quad Half-range sine: $b_n=\frac2L\int_0^L\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx$. Then
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{b_n=\frac2L\cdot\frac{L}{n\pi}\big(1-(-1)^n\big)=\frac{2}{n\pi}\big(1-(-1)^n\big)=\frac{4}{n\pi}\ (n\text{ odd}),\ 0\ (n\text{ even}).} \]
So $1=\frac4\pi\sum_{k\ge0}\frac{1}{2k+1}\sin\frac{(2k+1)\pi x}{L}$ on $0<x<L$.

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 6.}\quad Substituting $u=XT$ into $u_t=\alpha^2u_{xx}$ and dividing by $\alpha^2XT$ gives $\frac{T'}{\alpha^2T}=\frac{X''}{X}=-\lambda$. Thus
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\begin{gathered} X''+\lambda X=0,\ X(0)=X(L)=0\ \Rightarrow\ \lambda_n=\Big(\tfrac{n\pi}{L}\Big)^2,\ X_n=\sin\tfrac{n\pi x}{L}; \\ T'+\alpha^2\lambda_n T=0\ \Rightarrow\ T_n=e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2t}. \end{gathered}} \]

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 7.}\quad For $u=x^2-y^2$: $u_{xx}=2$, $u_{yy}=-2$, so
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\nabla^2u=2-2=0 \ (\text{harmonic}).} \]
For $v=e^x\sin y$: $v_{xx}=e^x\sin y$, $v_{yy}=-e^x\sin y$, so
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\nabla^2v=e^x\sin y-e^x\sin y=0 \ (\text{also harmonic}).} \]

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 8.}\quad By the second-order classification:
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{\begin{aligned} &u_t=\alpha^2u_{xx}: \ \text{parabolic (diffusion/heat)},\\ &u_{tt}=c^2u_{xx}: \ \text{hyperbolic (vibration/waves)},\\ &u_{xx}+u_{yy}=0: \ \text{elliptic (steady state).}\end{aligned}} \]

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 9.}\quad Separation gives $X_n=\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}$, $T_n=e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2t}$, so $u=\sum_n b_n\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}e^{-\alpha^2(n\pi/L)^2t}$. Matching $u(x,0)=100$,
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{b_n=\frac2L\int_0^L 100\sin\frac{n\pi x}{L}\,dx=\frac{200}{n\pi}\big(1-(-1)^n\big)=\frac{400}{n\pi}\ (n\text{ odd}),\ 0\ (n\text{ even}).} \]
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{u(x,t)=\frac{400}{\pi}\sum_{k\ge0}\frac{1}{2k+1}\sin\frac{(2k+1)\pi x}{L}\,e^{-\alpha^2((2k+1)\pi/L)^2t}\ \xrightarrow{t\to\infty}0.} \]

\medskip

\noindent\textbf{Solution 10.}\quad With initial velocity $g\equiv0$, d'Alembert's formula reduces to
\[ \textcolor{workpurple}{u(x,t)=\tfrac12\big[f(x-ct)+f(x+ct)\big].} \]
The initial shape splits into two half-amplitude copies, one traveling right at speed $c$ and one left at speed $c$; their sum reconstructs $f$ at $t=0$ and separates thereafter.

\medskip

\begin{conceptbox}[Unit Key Takeaway]
One idea organizes the whole unit: \textcolor{accentorange}{decompose into frequencies, then solve frequency by frequency}. Fourier series rebuild a function from orthogonal sines and cosines, with symmetry choosing the cosine or sine form and half-range extensions adapting it to a boundary. For PDEs, \textcolor{accentorange}{separation of variables} turns the equation into the Unit~17 \textcolor{accentorange}{eigenvalue BVP} plus a time ODE, superposition assembles the modes, and the \textcolor{accentorange}{initial condition} is fitted by a Fourier series. The \textcolor{accentorange}{Laplacian} is the shared spatial operator behind the heat, wave, and Laplace equations --- diffusion that smooths, vibration that propagates, and the harmonic steady state.
\end{conceptbox}

\end{document}
